text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table with legs abducted, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The assistant port is visible, positioned relative to the camera and robotic ports previously described.
The robotic surgical system is shown docked and ready for use in the adrenalectomy procedure.
The robotic surgical system is positioned parallel to the patient's limb on the lateral side, indicating the setup for the adrenalectomy procedure.
The robotic surgical system is positioned on the lateral side of the patient, demonstrating a side docking configuration.
The surgeon creates a pneumoperitoneum, inflating the abdominal cavity to provide a clear view for the robotic-assisted adrenalectomy.
The surgeon uses a finger to create a space in the abdominal cavity, establishing a pneumoperitoneum, which visibly inflates the abdomen.
The surgeon performs a retrograde dissection, carefully manipulating tissue layers to avoid damage to the surrounding structures.

The surgeon clamps large vessels and fulgurates smaller vessels while navigating within the femoral triangle. The sartorius muscle forms the lateral border, and the adductor muscle forms the medial border, with an accessory saphenous vein visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a clip to a vessel and cuts it, continuing the dissection along the saphenous vein towards the femoral vessels.
A surgical clip is being introduced through the assistant port, visible in the surgical field.
The assistant clips the vessels using a surgical clip applier, focusing on the vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field, identifying named branches originating from the inguinal ligament.

The surgeon identifies the superficial external pudendal artery, the superficial circumflex iliac artery, and the medial and lateral branches of the superficial iliac artery, highlighting their anatomical positions.

The surgeon identifies the superficial vessels, including the superficial external pudendal, superficial circumflex iliac, and medial and lateral branches of the superficial iliac, originating from the inguinal ligament.

The surgeon identifies the named superficial vessels, including the superficial circumflex iliac and the superficial branch of the superficial external pudendal, which are visible as perforators in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the fatty tissue, ensuring it is distinct from surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to manipulate and separate tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the border of the muscle, removing the surrounding fibro fatty tissue.
The surgeon observes a pulsating structure within the surgical field, indicating the presence of a blood vessel or similar anatomical feature.
The surgeon approaches the saphenous vein, carefully dissecting it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips the saphenous vein at its opening into the femoral vein and proceeds to cut it off.
A gauze is placed in the surgical field for hemostasis and to clear the area, visible through one of the ports.
The inguinal ligament is visible above, forming part of the anatomical triangle in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates the saphenous vein at multiple locations, encountering and ligating the accessory saphenous vein as well.
The completed dissection reveals the femoral region, with all relevant structures clearly exposed.
The surgeon removes the adrenalectomy specimen from the surgical field.
